speaker: interviewer
text: OK, this is tape number four with Jacques Rosenthal. So this gentleman had your wife and son staying with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, so I stayed in Prat for two days, and was sent where [INAUDIBLE] to the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your reunion like when you-- when you saw your wife? You hadn't seen her for months.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was happy to be alive. [LAUGHS] You can't imagine what impression it is on a person. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so after, when I went to the hospital in Perpignan, they send me back. There was no place. Only six weeks later-- six or seven weeks later-- they're called me from the hospital. And every day I was going to the doctor to get an injection. I still didn't realize what I have in me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a bullet. A bullet, 5 centimeters away from major organ in the back, and shrapnel-- shrapnel all over my left leg. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So in the beginning of August, they took out the bullet, and the shrapnel was too deep. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So afterward, I think I was a week or two weeks in the hospital, came back, and the town had lots of Jewish refugees-- escapees-- that was already [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in '40. And I start to look around, what's can I do? So I heard that they are building a big power station. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As a matter of fact, I find the letter from the man that hired me. That-- I-- and I went to the guy, and I told him my story.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, look-- they said, OK. And he told me this is a big project. He said, this is for hydraulic. It comes from the mountains, from high up in the mountains, and it's divided in 10 substations. So I'll assign you one substation, and also you'll have to help with taking out every day the help from camp Rivesaltes, the Spaniards. You may be in charge to go and pick them up and bring them back in the evening.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I accepted this job, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and I was working-- I was in charge of one station that was at [FRENCH] in France, and in [SPANISH] in Spain. So the Spaniards never wanted to go back to Spain. If they would catch them they would kill them right away. So no Spaniards was interested. So, but after a few-- a few weeks we had lots of help, but not professional. And in that time I heard it that to the camp they brought in 3,000 Jews from the city of Mannheim and surrounding. So I was thinking to myself, maybe we can find professional people. And what was the name of this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Camp Rivesaltes, Rivesaltes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I decided to see, but I needed permission from my chief-- Monsieur Demond. And he used to come out every two weeks only. Finally I-- he came there and I ask him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't say they're Jews. I said there's-- maybe within the 3,000 people, we can find a professional man that has knowledge in electricity or something. He said look, it's your job. You never ask me, I never heard anything, you do whatever you want. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And he was not Jewish. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took out-- I went to see a list with names. You couldn't-- they said bankers, but they never said that these are an electrician or a professional. So I picked about 10 or 12 names. Instead of taking 800 Spaniards, I took 790-- or whatever the number was-- to make up the 800.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the first day I brought them into the station and I opened the door from the other side-- I said, there's the [INAUDIBLE]. I didn't say anything else. I left them. In the evening, one was missing. So I brought them back to the camp. A few days later-- I changed every time. I changed from 10 to 12 and 15. After three or four days, I had maybe 20 people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I brought new people-- every time was new people. And one day I realized that there were three missing here. So my business, they didn't count the 800, so what's the difference how many comes back? So that was going on. Every day somebody was missing, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] and nothing happened until then. But one day it came from Perpignan, there came somebody from the Jewish committee, and they offered me if I'll take out those and those names-- for every name they'll give me so and so much. So I wasn't really-- I didn't know exactly, but I became frightened, and I stopped this. So until the end of '41, till I received the invitation from the American consulate in Marseilles, I left my job, already. And that was maybe December, or even before. You were helping these Jews that came from Mannheim. Did you know what was going on now throughout the rest of Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't know until I came to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it that you were able to be free as a Jew, even after you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not afraid of the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no, no-- afraid of the Germans. I mean, you were with the Polish army, but after that, how is it that you were able to be free? Your wife was in hiding, is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she wasn't. In the village we were not in hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: But she-- you had mentioned in our pre-interview why she came to Prat.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was assigned before the war. In case of if something happened, they assigned people with children that they have to flee from Paris. And she was assigned to Prat in that time. The girl that helped my wife home was the daughter of the mayor, and she was a help. She helped her in Paris. And my wife told me, if something happens, she'll go to Prat. And Prat is the same thing with the United States-- every state has a Springfield or Lincoln. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that's how I came to Prat. That's-- only Prat was open in the zone libre.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so you're in a free zone.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's [INAUDIBLE]. And there I met the man that helped, for two years already, 20,000 Spanish prisoners. The man was Pablo Casals, a well-known personality in the music world. He was a well-known cellist, and he was born in Catalonia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And since Franco came to power, he left Catalonia and never came back there. And he helped. And I having my contacts to the Quakers, and the Quakers moved away from France and they went to Lisbon. And in Lisbon, American Red Cross established a headquarters. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They had a man from Lisbon in the Red Cross, was a very close friends of mine, by the name of Roger Klein-- not a Jew, a Protestant from Kansas City-- and he reestablished for me the contact to the Quakers. And then I realized that the Quakers helped, also, Pablo Casals. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I didn't interfere in his business, and he'd never interfere to my business. So thanks to Pablo Casals that he helped so many Spaniards, indirectly he was a help for Jews. And that's why he asked my wife to dance for the 14 of July, in Prat, for the reception for Mr. Petain-- supposed to come to the city. And he-- that's his mental help, morally. He helped, he didn't want to give his program. He announced only the program. And in the program was my wife will have the first part in dancing the bolero, and he will announce his own program. And Pablo Casals, after that-- the first part was my wife was dancing. And he-- the first piece-- and the Germans were sitting in the audience. Luckily, Petain didn't show up.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Pablo Casals announced his first program, was called [NON-ENGLISH]. And the number one-- number two program was [NON-ENGLISH] by [NON-ENGLISH]. And the third program also was a Jewish theme by Ravel. This how he morally.

speaker: interviewee
text: He wasn't afraid of the Germans. [LAUGHS] That's why I like to mention Pablo Casals. And personally I couldn't-- I couldn't thank him then, I didn't realize. But in '62, '62 he came for a mass program to Berkeley. I had a chance to thank him-- in '62 or '63.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is-- and I kept working there in the station until the end of '41. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And on July 1, 1942, we came to the United States. Whatever happened with that letter you sent to your friend in New York? Did he ever get it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never received any notice from the-- from my friend. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This letter, he arranged all the papers for the-- when we came to the States. He was away. He was in Cuba in that time, and he came a-- three months later he came to the States and he told me that in '41, for Christmas-- between Christmas and New Years-- he was in Washington DC and arranged out papers. And that's when I had the news, where I didn't know for whom. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's the way-- and my daughter was born in '42, late '42. And her name is Renee Rae Rosenthal-- Rae Fax was her name-- Joseph Fax's wife. So we came to the Unites States. You have the pictures. [LAUGHS] We spent three years in New York City. We had offer when we came, July the 2ed when we came, they offered us to go to California. I said, no. He asked me, why no? I said, the war is going to finish tomorrow-- I want to go back to France.

speaker: interviewee
text: After being four months here I realized what it means-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just the Second Amendment to the first Constitution of the United States, that you are innocent until they find you guilty. I said, I'll never go back without being a citizen of something. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I'm glad to be here in '95 to tell you all about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did do when you came to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do after you came to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was broadcasting for Free France. I made $10 the whole week. So, but this-- I had an offer from my old company, Western Electric. The president what they had in France before the war, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when I came to France was the harbor commissioner in New York City in 1942. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to see him after that, and I tell him, I said, I'm looking for a job or something. And he said, no problem. And they arranged for me-- I interviewed with Western Electric in New Jersey someplace. I went to New Jersey, and yes they want to hire me [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] for $25 a week. But they said, are you a citizen? I said, no. They couldn't hire me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to-- Mr. Hodgkiss was the-- the harbor commissioner, and he was my president in 1939 of Western Electric. And he offered me-- he said let's try another-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] another branch of Western Electric. And also it didn't work out-- even before the interview they asked me if I'm a citizen.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I looked around and I find the-- a cousin of mine that came to this country in 1918 from Belgium, and he was in the diamond industry. And having mechanical experience, he said, I'll make from you-- I'll teach you in two or three months, but I can only pay you $14 a week. So I accepted. I [INAUDIBLE] for the guy for three months, I think, and when I felt that I have enough-- enough experience, I went out from him and I find another job for $16 a week.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I spent for the-- for almost three years in the diamond industry. And the diamond industry became part of defense-- for defense. Diamond tools became a new thing in the United States. So 50% of the production had to be for defense, and I was in charge for the defense production of diamonds. And I am here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you moved to Los Angeles when?

speaker: interviewee
text: July the 16th, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've been here ever since. What have you been doing here in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: I joined here the agricultural, from the grapes [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and from production and all kinds of things. Until-- till my retirement-- I retired in '72, I was involved in the production of California grapes, and traveled all over the world for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have seven grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Individually?

speaker: interviewer
text: However you'd like to.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're part of me. Even if I want, I cannot separate myself. [LAUGHS] I have one granddaughter that is married. The rest-- five graduated from universities, two are now in the university-- the first year in university-- and one of the two is already in the second year of university.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me the names of all of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest is Marla, her sister is Susan, her brother is Jason. My son has two families, and the two children from the number two family is Danielle and Brandon. My daughter is married to Dennis Ross. She's a graduate from Berkeley, and she has her master's from Berkeley, and her husband is a graduate. He has his PhD from the University of Washington State. And she has two daughters-- Michelle is married, and Susan is not married. And both granddaughters live in San Francisco.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that you would like to say to your children, or your grandchildren, or future generations about your experiences in Europe prior to coming to America during World War II?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm not a reformer and I'm not a preacher. I cannot tell anybody-- it's individual. I'll say what my father told me. My duty as a parent is to guide you, and you learn in school or you learn from life, and you do whatever you want. And I didn't do anything-- what's happened in my life-- by choice. So I cannot tell anybody what to do. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have wishful hopes or thinking, but this-- you cannot command those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are you religious now? Do you practice Judaism now, as you did when you were young?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I'm not-- the only thing, I'm not religious for one reason. When I reached-- if I am mature today-- when I reached my maturity and I realized the meaning what I was learning as a child by heart, I start to believe, really, in religion. Even a good thing has an end. And you take the religion-- our prayers and all the thing, the worshipping day in and day and night, to the God, to this God, to the other God-- it's absolutely obsolete for me. This, what I realized for me, but I don't say, I don't impose, and I don't tell anybody about this. This is, for me, what I realized. I believe I'm a Jew. I respect, so long that you respect me. That's all, and I don't have any message.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Jacques.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just mention pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to go and look at some of Jacques's pictures now.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Israel Rosenthal, and that picture was taken in Tel Aviv in the year 1936. This is my mother, Chana-- Anna Rosenthal. That picture was taken in Tel Aviv-- in that time, Palestine-- in 1936.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Solarska. This is myself, and the picture is taken in Paris in January or February in 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the name of--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my son's name is Claude Rosenthal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my name is Jacques Rosenthal. This is my wife, Celine-- born Zubika-- Rosenthal, and my son, Claude Rosenthal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is this? This is a newspaper picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture taken by the dated June the 30th, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aboard ship, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: But where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York City, in Staten Island. This is a picture taken in 1943, in February or March-- and between February and March, '43. This is me, Jacques Rosenthal, and my wife, Celine Rosenthal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Taken in Central Park West.

speaker: interviewer
text: In--

speaker: interviewee
text: New York City. This picture was taken late '43, and in front, the little girl is Renee Rosenthal. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The back of her is Cecile Speckter. The center is Claude Rosenthal, and to the right of my son is Gilbert Speckter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's the gentlemen?

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the back, this is me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where's this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Central Park West, also.
